serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Qingdao Clam Seafood Noodle is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of wheat noodles in seafood broth with clams, prawns, squid, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood noodle soup, Chinese-style — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), clams cooked (FDC ID 175177), mixed seafood, and seafood broth components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Chinese noodle dishes.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Qingdao Clam Seafood Noodle as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 40% cooked wheat noodles, 20% mixed seafood (clams, prawns, squid), 35% seafood broth, and 5% vegetables and condiments, using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries. Values are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Chinese seafood noodle dishes. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52); analogous estimate applied to Chinese seafood noodle dish dominated by wheat noodle component.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Qingdao Clam Seafood Noodle. GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category), which constitutes the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The seafood and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 57.6) / 100 ≈ 30.0, based on 450 g serving with 12.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seafood broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodle carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of 52 from wheat noodles is favourable, but the glycemic load per serving is approximately 30 g, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion or substitute with lower-carbohydrate noodle alternatives to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~48 mg per 100 g) from clams and prawns warrant caution for cardiovascular disease patients; however, the low saturated fat content (0.5 g per 100 g) and presence of omega-3-rich seafood are partially beneficial, and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Qingdao Clam Seafood Noodle (青岛蛤蜊海鲜面) is a Chinese noodle dish originating from Qingdao, a coastal city in Shandong Province, China, renowned for its fresh seafood. The dish features wheat noodles served in a rich, umami-laden seafood broth, typically garnished with fresh clams (蛤蜊, gélí), prawns, squid, and sometimes fish fillets, along with vegetables such as bok choy or spring onion. The broth is prepared by simmering seafood shells and aromatics including ginger, garlic, and spring onion, then seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The noodles are boiled separately and combined with the broth and seafood components at service. The texture profile includes tender wheat noodles, briny and slightly chewy clam meat, and a deeply savoury, clear-to-slightly-cloudy broth. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution (~7.2 g per 100 g) from the seafood components, and relatively low fat (~2.1 g per 100 g). The dominant nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the broth and soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~52) due to the wheat noodle base, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate to high. Clams are a notable source of vitamin B12 (~1.4 mcg per 100 g), iron (~1.8 mg per 100 g), and selenium (~18 mcg per 100 g). This dish is commonly served in Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often as a main course at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Qingdao Clam Seafood Noodle as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation methodology.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (USDA FoodData Central + FAO/INFOODS SEA); no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases for the components of this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases for the components of this dish. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA component data for boiled wheat noodles and cooked seafood; no added hydrogenated fats). Halal status is marked false as clams and mixed seafood preparation may not conform to halal certification requirements in all contexts; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. The dish is served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜) on Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 12.80. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles, which constitute the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile via weighted component analysis from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA.

Added multilingual local names and aliases for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized pinyin to satisfy G6 and Q8.
